nativeRange.habitat: Standing and slow-flowing freshwaters — lowland ponds, ditches, lakes, and slow rivers, typically over fine, organically rich substrates; tolerates calcareous and slightly brackish conditions.

decorPreferences: Fine, organically rich substrate (loam/sand/clay mix or planted-tank soil)
Open background space for long stems
Cool, well-lit setup — does well in unheated or coldwater aquaria

goodWith: Goldfish (in ponds, with caveats — may be uprooted)
avoidWith: Outdoor release of any kind — strictly invasive

summary: A hardy, cold-tolerant pondweed with distinctively serrated, wavy submerged leaves; well-suited to unheated aquaria and ponds, but a serious invasive species outside its native range — never release.
careNotes: INVASIVE-SPECIES WARNING: Potamogeton crispus is one of the most damaging aquatic invaders in North America (established in 40+ U.S. states), South America, and New Zealand, where it outcompetes native submerged vegetation. It MUST NEVER be released into any natural waterbody, and in many jurisdictions sale or possession is restricted or banned — including several New England states and parts of the Upper Midwest. Verify local regulations before purchasing. Disposal should be by drying completely and placing in household trash — never composting near watercourses. In the aquarium, Curly Pondweed is a forgiving cool-to-temperate plant with distinctive serrated, wavy submerged leaves (25–95 mm long, 5–12 mm wide) carried on flattened branching stems that can reach a meter or more in length. It is one of the few aquarium-suitable stem plants that tolerates both low light and low temperatures — wild populations have been documented surviving at 1–4°C and under ice — which makes it considerably more at home in unheated tanks and coldwater ponds than in tropical setups. It tends to decline above roughly 25°C and may go dormant in warmer conditions. Propagation is vegetative: the plant spreads through stem cuttings and through turions (dormant winter buds that form along the stem, drop to the substrate, and sprout new plants the following season), a reproductive strategy unusual among aquarium stem plants. Care is generally considered beginner-level: low-to-medium light, no CO2 supplementation required, and a nutrient-rich substrate. Growth tends to be fast under good conditions and can become vigorous enough to crowd out other plants, so periodic trimming is generally advised.
breedingNotes: Curly Pondweed propagates vegetatively through stem cuttings or by collecting dormant turions — the specialized winter buds that form along the stem, detach, and overwinter in the substrate before sprouting into new plants the following season. This turion-based dormancy strategy is unusual among aquarium stem plants and is one of the traits that makes the species so persistent in the wild. Flowers do appear in summer and are small and reddish, and seeds are produced, but seeds are rarely viable in the aquarium context.

primary.name: Wikipedia
primary.notes: Primary source swapped from Tropica to Wikipedia: the assigned Tropica URL (plantdetails/4457/4457) actually serves a Hydrocotyle verticillata page and Tropica does not catalog Potamogeton crispus. Wikipedia provided authoritative taxonomy, morphology, native range, and reproduction (turions).

additional.name: Glass Aqua
additional.notes: Aquarium-trade source confirming low-to-medium lighting, optional CO2, fast growth rate, nutrient-rich substrate preference, and beginner/easy difficulty; also confirms tolerance of low light, low temperatures, and slightly brackish water.

additional.name: Plantiary
additional.notes: Cross-checked aquarium parameters: temperature 10–24°C (50–75°F), pH 6.5–8.5, moderate-to-high light, nutrient-rich substrate, propagation by stem cuttings and turions.

additional.name: Royal Horticultural Society
additional.notes: Confirmed native to GB/Ireland, pH tolerance across acid/neutral/alkaline, H7 hardiness (survives below -20°C), and ultimate spread of 1–1.5 m over 5–10 years.

additional.name: USGS NAS / Wisconsin DNR / Washington Dept of Ecology
additional.notes: Authoritative for invasive-species status in North America: established in 40+ U.S. states, priority management species in the Great Lakes and Midwest; survives at 1–4°C and under ice.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Curly Pondweed is a regulated invasive species in many regions — sale, possession, or transport is restricted or banned in much of North America (multiple U.S. states, several New England jurisdictions) and elsewhere. Verify local regulations before acquiring, and NEVER release into any natural waterway.
